articles.title: The World in Flames
articles.author: Jared Levy
articles.description: A couple is stranded on an island when a fire engulfs Sydney, Australia.
articles.intro: Jared Levy is a writer based in Philadelphia, PA. His work appears in various publications and he holds an MFA from Warren Wilson College. In this story, a couple named Tom and Frances are on an island in Sydney Harbor, Australia.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the resolution contributes to the story’s overall meaning.
articles.permissions: "The World in Flames" by Jared Levy. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: I kept a routine. Every morning, I left the tent and walked to the island’s communal kitchen. I prepared coffee by boiling water over an open flame, and then I mixed in used grounds. I strained the black juice through a sock, since the remaining coffee filters were reduced to pulp. Once I made the coffee, I brought a mug with me to the other side of the island where there were Adirondack chairs and a view of what remained of the Sydney Opera House.
I sat and watched the city in a perpetual blaze. Flames climbed up the Opera House’s concrete ribs. There were barely any buildings left. It was mostly ash and smoke.

I’d lost count of the days. I had no more understanding of the fire than when it started. It was a permanent fixture in my life, or what was left of it. Whether I was living or surviving was a question I often asked myself and when I did, I thought about Frances.

I like to think of us as longtime islanders. Frances was from Sydney, and I was from Manhattan. We’d met a few months earlier, she’d just graduated from college and was visiting the city. I had just broken up with the woman I had dated since the end of my senior year. I didn’t realize until it was over how much that relationship defined me, gave me meaning. I was stunned by how lost I felt, caught in a vacuum of days spent bar hopping or binge watching shows on streaming services. True Crime. Survivor Series. Anything but romantic comedies.

I’d gone to my favorite bar, Hardy’s, because I liked that it was always loud there, and people were jovial, even if I wouldn’t be, and drinking alone you could always lose yourself without feeling like you completely lost yourself. In walked Frances wearing a black velvet dress. It was an odd choice, given that it was summer, but the dress contrasted beautifully with her red hair and piercing blue eyes. I watched her walk from the door to a table next to mine and, miraculously, I found my voice:
Where are you from? What do you do? What did you study?

She’d studied English, and wanted to write children's books. I told her that was funny, because I was a paralegal, but I wanted to write too. She spoke softly under the throb of music, and I leaned in closer to hear her. I was inches from her pale skin and thick Australian accent.

Later that night, I asked if I could kiss her, and she nodded. We kissed, and held each other like no one was watching. I promised her a place to stay for the night, no funny business, but she said she had to go back to her hostel. She was returning home the next day.

On a whim, I asked if we could be pen pals. She agreed to the idea. She scrawled her address on the back of her receipt. Writing came naturally to us. We communicated more freely in letters than we had in person:
I can’t stop thinking about you, I wrote.
It would have been cool if we had more time to get to know each other.
Why can’t we?
We could run away together.
Yes! Somewhere remote. Like an island.
You could visit me.

So I planned a trip. I had the vacation time. Frances suggested a place for us to stay on Cockatoo Island. It was a former prison turned tourist destination. The website showed pictures of 19th century stone structures next to modern conveniences like porta potties. We could book a tent and explore the island. I loved the idea.
The morning after we arrived, Frances woke me up screaming, “Sydney’s on fire!”

Even before I left the tent, there was an extreme quality to the light that filtered through the canvas. The stench was powerful. I didn’t know what to picture, but I walked out and saw it for myself.
Across the water, fire spread in horrible, violent towers reaching upward. The sky was a deep orange, and the sun shrank to a speck.

There were only two other couples on the island. They were much older than us, in their 50s and 60s, the Ruths and the Johnsons. We all tried our phones, but there was no signal. No television either.
We waited for the ferry to come and get us, but the ferry never came. Boats sped by, but they didn’t stop.
The other couples clutched onto one another and cried. Frances and I stood together and stared across the water in silence.
For days we stayed in our tents, rationed food from the communal kitchen, or got together and prayed. I didn't believe in God, but praying seemed like the right thing to do.

Weeks passed. The air was thick with smoke. We used t-shirts to cover our noses and mouths, but the taste and smell of burning wood and chemicals was still suffocating. Then months. Frances stopped leaving the tent. I tried to comfort her, but she barely spoke. I remembered her talking about an ex-boyfriend when we met in New York, and I wondered if he was more than an ex, if he was who she mourned. I tried to make the best of things. She gave me nothing.
The fire never stopped burning. Near the year mark of our abandonment, I looked back from my seat in the Adirondack chair and saw Joe, one of the Johnsons, standing behind me.
“Hey, Joe.”
“Hey, Tom.”
“What’s going on?”
I nodded towards Sydney.
“Looking at the fire. It’s weird that it’s beautiful. You forget there were people in there.”
He paused and scanned the landscape.
“I’ve been thinking that as well.”
“I was obsessed with fire as a kid.”
I kicked at the arid ground, mostly dirt now.
“Me too. Growing up, we took trips into the outback. My father was part Tasmanian and taught me all this cool stuff, like how to start a fire with a pocket compass.”
“You think your dad’s OK?”
He nodded. “He died before the fire. My parents were older. They passed away last year. Sounds sad, but it’s not, really. It was their time. It’s my kids I worry about.”
I didn’t know what to say. I didn’t have any children. My family was back in the States, or was, last I knew. We rarely spoke.
“What happened to Frances? We haven’t seen her in a while.”
“She won’t leave the tent. I can’t convince her to go outside.”
“People don’t know what to do in these situations. These situations . There hasn’t been anything like this since the Bible.”
We looked back at the flames.
I realized I was late bringing coffee back to Frances. I worried it was getting cold. In New York she told me she loved breakfast in bed, and I tried to do that one small thing for her every day.
I picked up an empty can of beans by the chair and threw it out into the water. “I have to head back. Later, Joe.”
“Later, Tom.”
It was quiet when no one else was around. I didn’t hear any animals. I hadn't seen any birds. This was Cockatoo island. Where were the cockatoos? Where did they go?
Inside the tent, Frances was lying awake in our shared sleeping bag.
“You’re up,” I said.
“Yeah,” she said.
“Joe was asking about you,” I said. “Maybe it’d be nice to get out and socialize.”
“I don’t want to see it,” she said, flatly. “It’s enough that I can smell it.”

On the night we arrived, Frances and I explored the island. We brought our bags and walked past riveted metal protruding out of the ground. I thought they looked like found sculptures. The sky was cloudy. Sydney glimmered across the water.

We climbed onto old cranes and swam in a slipway. The place was an industrial graveyard for the prison that once stood there. I was curious about the history and excited for this time with Frances. I’d felt an instant connection with her in New York. Each moment near her felt charged with possibility.

We found the prison’s old communal kitchen with a stockpile of non-perishables and sat at a table next to a decommissioned fireplace. From my backpack, I pulled out a bottle of whiskey that I had bought in Sydney. I poured some into a couple of ceramic mugs I found. We took the mugs along with us as we ventured further into the island’s dark corridors and wandered around the former buildings.
The entrances to the cells were locked. The steel bars which had kept people trapped were rusted but still strong. I started pretending there were escaped convicts hiding in the shadows.
“Stop it!” Frances said, laughing.
“Are you scared?” I said.
“It’s creepy in here,” she replied.
And then, more seriously, “I don’t like surprises.”

I thought we were picking up where we left off, but she didn’t say much after that or when we got back to the tent. It wasn’t clear what she wanted. I moved in to kiss her and she kissed me, but she didn’t touch me. She just lay there.
“What’s wrong?”
“Nothing.”
“It must be something. We planned this trip for months. It seemed like you wanted me here. You wanted to be with me.”
“I did.”
“Then why won’t you touch me?”
She didn’t respond.
“I miss the person from our letters,” I said, and left the tent.

It was winter and cold at night. Our tent was one of dozens, but the rest were silent. I wasn’t sure if anyone had heard us talking. I wasn’t even sure if there was anyone else on the island. It wasn't until later that I met the Ruths and the Johnsons.
It was off season, an off night.
I went back into the tent and told Frances I wouldn’t kiss her again. I’d stay on the other side of the tent. And then the next morning, the fire started.
“It’s only a matter of time,” Frances said to me the morning I was late with the coffee. “We can’t live like this much longer. We’re trapped.”
“What can we do?” I said. “I’m trying to survive.”
Frances didn’t respond.

I left the tent and went for a run. I did chin ups on the riveted metal. I wondered if it would be better if I moved into another tent. Left Frances alone. But who did I have left? Who did she? It felt painfully American, but I thought I owed her my hope. My optimism. I was the one who made the trip.

I studied the history of the island written on placards. It was one mile long and a half mile wide. The cockatoos fled when they cut the trees for logging. There were four recorded attempts to escape from the island when it was a prison. Three were successful.
I walked back to the tent. There was light rain and fog surrounding the island. Sydney was invisible through the plumes of smoke.
I called out to Frances and she stuck her head out of the tent.
“It’s raining,” she said, and smiled. I couldn’t remember the last time she’d smiled.
I coaxed her out of the tent and we played a game of Marco Polo in the slipway. I searched for her through tufts of gray smoke. She laughed like it was the first time we met.
“Why can’t it always be like this?” I asked.
“Why can’t you be happy with how it is now?” Frances said.
I splashed her and ushered her back into the tent. I ran my hand down her arm and told her that I loved her.
“Don’t say that,” she said. “This isn’t a time for love.”
“Why not?” I said. “Isn’t this as good a time as any?”
She didn’t respond.

I lay down on the sleeping bag, laced my fingers behind my head and looked up at the ceiling. Her history was a mosaic of missing pieces. So was mine, but I was willing to share. How I got into a fight with my family before moving to New York. How I stopped returning their calls and regretted it, now more than ever. I wanted to feel like she might care.

But she stayed silent. She rested her head on my arm. I didn’t mention my family. Instead, I told her about the history of the island. The missing birds. The attempted escapes. The stories I used to write. Funny little stories loosely fictionalizing my life. She said she couldn’t write children’s stories any more.
“How about we rest?” I said and closed my eyes.
There was still fog when I woke up, but it was lighter. I gently lifted Frances off my arm and walked out of the tent. I went to the edge of the island and looked out at Sydney.
I could still see the flames lick the fog. I started to cry, not for Frances, but for myself.
I turned around and headed in the opposite direction.
We all wondered about the cause of the fire. We got together during the first nights of the fire and speculated.

The Ruths were religious. They were convinced it was God’s revenge on humanity. They were the ones who encouraged prayer and they were the ones with speeches about how the fire was payment for mankind’s sin, talk which I thought brought on Frances’ dark moods. She was Catholic, and I took that to mean that she was conditioned to feel guilt. I was conditioned too with my Jewish upbringing, but in a different way. Frances got quiet and went inward. I wouldn’t stop talking and moving, unable to sit still with my guilt.

The Johnsons had their theories about the fire — ecological catastrophe, political coup — but they seemed unconcerned with blame and even less concerned with sadness. They looked spry and were generous with their affection towards one another. In their company, I nudged Frances playfully, but she wasn’t amused.
Their stance seemed odd, but over time, it made sense: there’s only what is. Resisting causes suffering, so why not accept?
I hoped Frances and I could be like the Johnsons.
When I walked back to the tent, Frances was gone. Her clothes were strewn on the floor. The sleeping bag was pushed to the side. I wondered where she went.
I ran past the ancient buildings. I checked the porta potties. I searched the ground for tracks, and yelled out her name, but there was no reply.
The Johnsons came out of their tent.
“What's wrong?” Joe asked.
“Have you seen Frances?” I asked.
“She told us she was going for a dip on the Sydney side,” Joe said.
That was where the convicts escaped.
I took off running.

The first few days, I saw people in Sydney run into the water. I couldn’t hear anything, because of the distance, but I watched as blurs — people, maybe animals, too — ran from the fire into the dark. Eventually, piles formed on the shore. Figures didn’t run into the water anymore. It was just the fire.
When I came out of the woods onto the Sydney-side beach, I saw Frances swimming towards the fire.
“Frances!” I yelled.
She kept swimming.
I threw my clothes off and dove into the cold water. She was ahead of me, but I felt that I could catch her.
I got out to where I had seen her last. I thought I could see her shadow, down beneath me. I flipped up my legs and dove down.

As I went deeper, I could swear I saw her black velvet dress in the darkness below, the one she’d worn the night we met in New York. I reached for its hem and it glinted in the light from the fires reflected through the waves.
I came up gasping for air. It was raining again. I could hear the hiss of water connecting with fire.
I tread water and closed my eyes. For one small moment, everything felt OK. But then I saw the dress again in my mind’s eye and saw her sinking shadow.
I tread water, waited, and then tried my luck, one more time.

articles.title: Dam Revives Aral Sea and Nearby Communities in Kazakhstan
articles.author: Morning Edition, NPR
articles.description: NPR discusses the positive impacts a dam has had on the landscape and surrounding communities.
articles.intro: The Aral Sea is an inland body of water in Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan and was once one of the four biggest lakes in the world. In this informational text, NPR discusses what caused the sea's retreat and what actions are being taken to bring it back to life.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how changes to the Aral Sea have impacted surrounding communities.
articles.permissions: © 2007 David Stern. News report titled "Dam Revives Aral Sea and Nearby Communities" was originally broadcast on Morning News on October 1, 2007, and is used with the permission of NPR. Any unauthorized duplication is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: STEVE INSKEEP, HOST : Now in the former Soviet Republic of Kazakhstan, government and Western officials have reversed what is considered one of mankind's greatest ecological catastrophes. It’s the drying up of the Aral Sea. That was once the world’s fourth largest inland body of water. Thanks to a recently built dam, part of the sea is returning and with it, life is returning to surrounding communities.
David Stern reports.

DAVID STERN : This is what hope sounds like to hundreds of thousands of people living in west central Kazakhstan. It is a sound of water pouring to the sluices of the Kok-Aral Dam, part of an $86 million World Bank-funded project to bring life back to the Aral Sea. The sea is a catchword for one of the Soviet Union’s most spectacular failures of trying to bend nature’s will to the demands of a glorious Soviet future.
Soviet officials tapped heavily into the main river supplying the Aral to make the desert bloom and irrigate water-thirsty cotton fields.

By the 1990s, the Aral had lost more than half of its surface and 80 percent of its volume. Towns that stood on the water’s edge found themselves as far as 150 miles away. Maraturu Muratov(ph), a local doctor, grew up in the main Kazakh port city of Aralsk, and remembers the sea’s abrupt departure.
MARATURU MURATOV : We feel water — I don’t see it disappear, we feel it, we know it about it, you know. We know because it was very fast in our eyes, before our eyes, you know.

STERN : With the Aral’s vanishing came a host of economic, ecological and health problems. Fishing communities also dried up. The region suffered massive emigration. The storm saturate with salt and pesticides partially left over from the exposed seabed swept the land. Instances of tuberculosis, known as a poor person’s disease, skyrocketed. But all these began to change five years ago. The sea shrank so much that it split into two parts, a larger portion in the neighboring Uzbekistan and a smaller in Kazakhstan. Planners hit upon the idea of damming the water flowing from the smaller to the larger. The project succeeded. The lesser sea has risen 12 feet, and water has crept back to just a dozen miles from places that long ago forgot the sound of waves. Rain now falls regularly.

STERN : I’m standing in the middle of an endless sea of grass next to a rusted out trawler named for the Ukrainian city of Kiev. Cows and camels graze around me. The sea began to retreat since 30 years ago. Now, they say, it may come back.

STERN : In the gritty Kazakh village of Jambul, population 753, people are getting ready for the water’s homecoming. Juxaby Tulumjan(ph) says that after a planned second dam is completed, his town will once again be nestled on the Aral shores. People are returning. Houses are going up. And in preparation for the numbers, he believes, will flock here, Juxaby is building a cafe and bathhouse. Hope, he says, has returned to Jambul.
JUXABY TULUMJAN : (Through translator) We feel it in our lives. Everyone believes here when the water appears, everything will be good. We have water, we have electricity.

STERN : Kazakhstan’s portion of the sea has become less salty, and schools of fish unseen for decades now ply its waters. With them, the local fishing industry is swelling. Idenvex Arsena(ph) fishes regularly on top of the newly built dam and earns close to $100 for three days work, a king’s ransom in these parts. With the money, he says, he supports his wife and two small children.
IDENVEX ARSENA : (Through translator) I will also fish in the winter. This is the only work there is. I've been jobless since December.

STERN : The local tuberculosis clinic in Aralsk is far less crowded than it was just a few years ago. Outside in the dusty courtyard, however, the remaining patients say that though life is improving, it is far from ideal. One of their number, a tall real thin 20-year-old who simply gives his name as Togat(ph), says that if the sea returns, he hopes he can finally find a job. For the moment, he and his colleagues wile away the days under the merciless Kazakh sun, singing folk songs.
TOGAT : (Singing) (Kazakh spoken)
STERN : For NPR News, I’m David Stern in Aralsk, Kazakhstan.

options.label: Humans depleted the water in the Aral Sea, causing an imbalance in the environment that negatively impacted surrounding communities.
Officials have been able to fully restore the Aral Sea and surrounding communities due to their construction of a dam.
The Aral Sea was drying up naturally on its own, but because of human interferences, it is experiencing a revival.

The greed of the agricultural industry resulted in officials' decision to redirect water from the Aral Sea to crops. E. It is unlikely that the communities surrounding the Aral Sea will ever be as successful as they were before the disaster. F. Communities around the Aral Sea are seeing great improvement in life now that measures have been taken to bring the sea back.

articles.title: Cave Art May Have Been Handiwork Of Neanderthals
articles.author: Christopher Joyce
articles.description: Christopher Joyce discusses the discovery of cave art that may have originated from Neanderthals.
articles.intro: In this article, Christopher Joyce discusses a new discovery regarding cave art in Spain that is believed to be the work of Neanderthals. Neanderthals were a type of early human being that existed long ago in Europe.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why scientists believe that the cave art belongs to Neanderthals.
articles.permissions: ©2018 National Public Radio, Inc. News report titled "Cave Art May Have Been Handiwork Of Neanderthals" by Christopher Joyce was originally published on NPR.org on February 22, 2018, and is used with the permission of NPR. Any unauthorized duplication is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: Tens of thousands of years ago, the first artists painted images on the walls of caves. They collected, painted and ground holes in shells, presumably to wear. It was the very first art, created by what we call “modern humans,” or Homo sapiens.

Except, it turns out that some of that cave art may have been created by Neanderthals — our ancient and, by evolutionary standards, failed cousins. At least, that’s what a team of scientists is now claiming.

The painted caves were discovered in Spain. The walls were the canvasses, and the paintings are bold and clearly not some kind of smeary accident. The paint used was red ochre, from soil mixed with water.

One geometric design looks like part of a ladder, forming rectangles. There are stencils where someone pressed a hand up against the wall and then apparently blew liquid ochre over it. Someone painted swirls of bright red dots and patches onto flowing curtains of stalactites that hang from the cave ceilings.
Most of this work has long been known and attributed to humans, who originally came from Africa and are believed to have arrived in Spain about 45,000 years ago.

But here’s the catch. New tests on the rock and calcium carbonate that formed over parts of the ochre show that they were painted 65,000 years ago. That’s about 20,000 years before the first modern humans got there.

“The only species that were around at that time were Neanderthals,” explains Alistair Pike, an archaeologist from the University of Southampton in England who was part of the team that did the work. “So, therefore, the paintings must’ve been made by them.”

Pike says the original technique to determine the age of the paintings was used decades ago and wasn’t very reliable. It measured how much carbon was in the rock, which is prone to error. So these scientists employed a different dating technique using radioisotopes of uranium and thorium. That technique showed the paintings were much older than first thought. That turned the conventional wisdom, that only modern humans made art, on its head.
“We’re over the moon,” says Pike. “This has taken us 10 years to get to this point.”

Neanderthals are thought to have evolved in Asia and Europe from a common ancestor of humans and Neanderthals. Their bones were first discovered in Europe in the 19th century and they were as close to us physically as any known human relative, but Neanderthals were thought to have been brutish and stupid subspecies of modern humans. Recent genetic evidence indicates that they did breed with modern humans who came over from Africa.
But, art? That requires abstract, symbolic thinking — recreating something real, like a pattern or a landscape, with symbols.

“We have examples of pigment use and cave paintings way outside of Europe [in Africa], and we have them in Europe, clearly demonstrated to be of Neanderthal origin,” says Dirk Hoffmann, a physicist with the Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology and an expert on the dating on the paintings in Spain. He says this new analysis shows that art evolved independently in both places.
Hoffmann suggests that making these paintings may well have required planning, organization, perhaps even language, though he acknowledges that’s speculation.

And clearly, some of the paintings required hearty determination. “They’re actually hidden away,” he says. “If you want to see them you walk into the deepest, darkest part of the cave and you actually have to lie down and crawl to the cave wall and look up, and they’re actually on the ceiling in little overhangs.”

In a fourth cave, the scientists also found another pile of evidence pointing to Neanderthals: painted shells, some with a single hole drilled through them, perhaps to accommodate a string. The shells lay in a seaside cave and date back about 115,000 years — far earlier than the first known sign of modern humans in Spain.
“I think we should accept them as part of us. They are part of our lineage, they are human, they’re just a different human population,” says Alistair Pike.

The results are published this week in the journals Science and Science Advances . Whether the scientific community will give Neanderthals final credit depends on whether the latest dating technique holds up to scrutiny. These techniques give a range of possible dates, rather than an exact time. And there was certainly overlap between modern humans and Neanderthals in Europe. If new evidence shows that humans actually arrived earlier than scientists now think, well, that’s the pattern of science.

options.label: New dating technology suggests that Neanderthals are responsible for some of the first cave art, rather than modern humans, as previously believed.
Scientist have always underestimated the capabilities of Neanderthals and it has affected their scientific observations.
If the new discovery regarding Neanderthals proves to be true, it would suggest that they were capable of more complex thought.

The discovery of Neanderthal cave art has helped scientists develop a better understanding of their daily life. E. Neanderthals' complex cave art suggest that they were of high intelligence and likely more closely related to modern humans. F. While Neanderthals were previously believed to be the first cave artists, a recent study has proven that modern humans arrived earlier.
